title: Production Hardening
description:
## Hardening a Production Realm

You are securing a Keycloak deployment before go-live. Which of the following is a **legitimate production hardening step**?

options.text: Properly configure the **hostname and TLS** (terminating TLS or running behind a reverse proxy with correct proxy/hostname settings) and define **specific, non-wildcard redirect URIs** for each client
Run with **`start-dev`** because it boots faster and skips strict checks
Set client redirect URIs to **`*`** so any new app can plug in without a config change
**Disable TLS** between the proxy and Keycloak since traffic stays inside the firewall

explanation: Real hardening means: configure the **hostname** and **TLS** correctly — either terminate TLS at Keycloak or run behind a reverse proxy with the right **proxy headers and hostname** settings — and lock down **redirect URIs** to **specific, non-wildcard** patterns per client. (Other good steps in the same spirit: short access-token lifespans, restricting/securing the **master realm** admin, key rotation, and enabling **brute force detection**.) (a) is correct. (b) `start-dev` is for development and *weakens* security — the opposite of hardening. (c) wildcard `**`/`*` redirect URIs enable **open-redirect** and token-theft attacks and are a top misconfiguration to avoid. (d) disabling TLS exposes tokens and credentials on the internal network; 'inside the firewall' is not a substitute for transport encryption.

hints.title: Tighten, don't loosen
hints.body: Anything that adds convenience by widening trust (wildcards, dev mode, plaintext) is the wrong answer. Hardening narrows redirect URIs, enforces TLS, and reduces token/admin exposure.
